notes: Reproduced on all tested versions (2.88.3, 3.119.2, and main source). The attached image.jpeg is actually an AVIF image (magic bytes: ftypavif) with a misleading .jpeg extension. Skia does not include an AVIF codec, so SKCodec.Create returns null with CodecResult=Unimplemented. This is expected behavior. The reporter's image is valid but not a JPEG; it opens in other viewers because they support AVIF natively. Additionally, SKBitmap.Decode(byte[]) throws ArgumentNullException instead of returning null when codec creation fails — this is a secondary bug.

Inspect the image file format — reveals it is AVIF, not JPEG
Create console repro project with SkiaSharp 2.88.3 and write test code exercising SKBitmap.Decode, SKCodec.Create, and SKData paths

Same behavior as 2.88.3 — all decode methods return null with CodecResult=Unimplemented.
Same behavior on main branch source — AVIF format remains unsupported.

corrections.upstream: JPEG codec limitation — specific JPEG image cannot be decoded
corrections.corrected: The file is not a JPEG at all — it is an AVIF image with a .jpeg extension. Skia does not include an AVIF codec, so this is expected behavior (Unimplemented) rather than a JPEG codec limitation.

actionability.reason: The attached file is an AVIF image misnamed as .jpeg. Skia does not support AVIF decoding, so returning null with CodecResult=Unimplemented is expected. This is not a bug in SkiaSharp but an unsupported format.

actions.description: Post explanation that the image is AVIF, not JPEG

proposedResponse.body:
Thanks for the report and the attached image!

We investigated this and found that the attached `image.jpeg` file is actually an **AVIF image** (ISO Media, AVIF format) with a misleading `.jpeg` file extension. You can verify this by checking the file's magic bytes — they start with `ftypavif` rather than the JPEG magic bytes `FFD8FF`.

Skia (the underlying native library) does not include an AVIF codec, which is why `SKCodec.Create` returns `null` with `SKCodecResult.Unimplemented` — it correctly identifies that the format is not supported.

The image opens in Windows Photos, browsers, and other viewers because those applications have built-in AVIF support.

**Tested versions:**
| Version | Result |
|---------|--------|
| 2.88.3 (reported) | ❌ NULL — CodecResult: Unimplemented |
| 3.119.2 (latest) | ❌ NULL — CodecResult: Unimplemented |
| main (source) | ❌ NULL — CodecResult: Unimplemented |

**Workaround:** Pre-process AVIF images with a library that supports AVIF (e.g., [SixLabors.ImageSharp](https://github.com/SixLabors/ImageSharp) or [LibHeifSharp](https://github.com/0xC0000054/libheif-sharp)) and convert to PNG/JPEG before passing to SkiaSharp.

**Note:** The `SKBitmap.Decode(byte[])` overload throws `ArgumentNullException` instead of returning null when the codec fails — this could be improved to return null consistently like the other overloads.

**Tip:** You can use `SKCodec.Create(stream, out SKCodecResult result)` to get diagnostic information when decoding fails.

workarounds: Pre-process AVIF images with a library that supports AVIF (e.g., SixLabors.ImageSharp or LibHeifSharp) and convert to PNG/JPEG before passing to SkiaSharp
Use SKCodec.Create(stream, out SKCodecResult result) to get diagnostic error codes when decoding fails
